skill.name: deploying-cloud-k8s
skill.description: Guides production deployments to cloud Kubernetes with CI/CD, Helm, GitOps, observability, security controls, and troubleshooting patterns for AKS, GKE, and DOKS.
skill.summary: Deploy and troubleshoot cloud Kubernetes applications with CI/CD, Helm, GitOps, and production readiness checks.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows most are Markdown deployment examples rather than executable malicious code. No prompt-injection text or credential exfiltration intent was found. The skill has medium operational risk because it teaches production cluster commands and includes secret-handling and RBAC examples that should be used with care.

medium_findings.title: Command-Line Secret Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends passing deployment secrets through Helm --set values in CI/CD examples. This is common but can expose secrets through logs, shell history, process arguments, or release metadata if masking and storage controls are weak.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly show secrets passed to Helm with --set. The pattern is not malicious, but it is a real operational security risk in deployment automation.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Example RBAC Access to Secrets
medium_findings.description: The production pattern grants an application role get and list access to secrets. This can exceed least privilege and increase blast radius if an application service account is compromised.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The YAML grants secret access in an example role. It may be legitimate for some controllers, but it is risky as generic application guidance.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Code Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick and external command alerts are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced examples for kubectl, helm, openssl, and Python verification. The files do not automatically execute these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are documentation examples or a local structure verifier. scripts/verify.py imports subprocess but does not call it or execute external commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Deployment Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to example application endpoints, internal Kubernetes services, GitHub repositories, ArgoCD manifests, and Helm chart repositories. No evidence shows unauthorized outbound requests by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible deployment configuration examples. I did not find code that sends credentials or telemetry to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Kustomize Relative Path Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../../base path is a normal Kustomize overlay reference from a production overlay to a base manifest directory. It is not used to read arbitrary files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding manifest shows a standard Kustomize resources entry. No user-controlled path or filesystem read is present.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Alerts Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts are not supported by semantic evidence. The only relevant example tells users to avoid base64 password generation issues and use hex output instead.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited skill text discusses password character compatibility, not weak hashing or encryption. The verify script line is ordinary text validation and contains no cryptographic code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Cluster Modification Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes kubectl and helm commands that can alter real Kubernetes clusters. These commands are expected for the skill purpose but require user review and correct context selection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real operational commands for Kubernetes and Helm. They are not hidden or automatic, but they can affect production infrastructure if run in the wrong context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Names and Secret Sources in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references DATABASE_URL, BETTER_AUTH_SECRET, GRAFANA_PASSWORD, and API_KEY as placeholders or secret keys. These are not real secrets in the repository, but they indicate sensitive deployment flows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders and GitHub secret references, not embedded credentials. The risk depends on how users adapt the examples in their CI and cluster configuration.

content.user_title: Deploy Kubernetes Apps to Cloud Clusters
content.value_statement: Cloud Kubernetes deployments fail when build settings, secrets, and cluster architecture are misaligned. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a practical checklist for CI/CD, Helm, GitOps, and production debugging.
actual_capabilities: Explains build-time versus runtime variables for Next.js deployments.
Guides Docker build platform selection for arm64 and amd64 clusters.
Provides GitHub Actions patterns for path-filtered image builds.
Documents Helm deployment commands and common parsing failures.
Covers GitOps, observability, RBAC, network policies, HPA, and PDB examples.
Lists Kubernetes troubleshooting commands for pods, events, logs, and image errors.
limitations: Does not provision cloud accounts, clusters, registries, or DNS records by itself.
Examples use placeholders that must be adapted before production use.
Does not replace a review of secret handling, RBAC scope, and compliance controls.
Focuses on Kubernetes deployment patterns, not application code design.

use_cases.title: Launch a Production Deployment
use_cases.description: Prepare a cloud Kubernetes release with matching image architecture, runtime configuration, Helm values, and deployment checks.

use_cases.title: Repair a Failed Rollout
use_cases.description: Diagnose ImagePullBackOff, CrashLoopBackOff, platform mismatch, Helm parsing, and redirect configuration issues.

use_cases.title: Standardize Delivery Workflows
use_cases.description: Create repeatable GitHub Actions, GitOps, observability, security, and resilience patterns for service teams.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a cloud Kubernetes deployment checklist for my app. Include build platform, secrets, Helm, and rollback checks.

prompt_templates.title: Fix My Failed Pod
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to diagnose my Kubernetes deployment failure. The pod status is [status], the namespace is [namespace], and the latest event is [event].

prompt_templates.title: Review My CI/CD Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my GitHub Actions Kubernetes pipeline for build args, image tags, platform matching, and secret handling risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design Production Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a production Kubernetes delivery model with GitOps, observability, network policies, RBAC, HPA, PDB, and safe secret management.

output_examples.input: I need to deploy a Next.js app and API to GKE with GitHub Actions.
output_examples.output: A deployment plan that separates public build variables from runtime secrets, checks cluster CPU architecture, builds matching images, and deploys with Helm.

output_examples.input: My pod shows ImagePullBackOff after a release.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting sequence covering image tag verification, registry access, path-filtered build skips, image platform mismatch, and relevant Kubernetes event checks.

output_examples.input: We need production readiness patterns for Kubernetes services.
output: RBAC and network policy review points

best_practices: Confirm the active Kubernetes context and namespace before running deployment commands.
Store production secrets in managed secret systems and minimize command-line exposure.
Review generated manifests for least privilege, resource limits, probes, and rollback behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder domains, repository URLs, or secret names into production unchanged.
Do not grant application service accounts broad access to list Kubernetes secrets by default.
Do not deploy images before verifying tag consistency and cluster CPU architecture.

faq.question: Which cloud Kubernetes platforms does this skill mention?
faq.answer: It mentions AKS, GKE, and DOKS, and its examples use generic Kubernetes, Helm, GitHub Actions, and ArgoCD patterns.

faq.question: Can it run deployments automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must decide which commands to run in their own environment.

faq.question: Does it include real credentials?
faq.answer: No evidence of real credentials was found. It uses placeholder secret names and GitHub Actions secret references.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill handles production deployment workflows and secrets. Most static alerts are false positives, but operational misuse can affect real clusters.

faq.question: Is it useful for troubleshooting?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers pod status checks, events, logs, image pull errors, platform mismatch, Helm parsing, and redirect issues.

faq.question: What should teams review before publishing generated changes?
faq.answer: Review secrets, RBAC permissions, network policies, image tags, cluster context, namespaces, resource limits, probes, and rollback plans.
